solutions.title: Tutorial
solutions.content:
1431D - Used Markers
Suppose, we used $$$k$$$ markers. It means that there were $$$k - 1$$$ indices $$$1  \lt  i_1  \lt  i_2  \lt  \dots  \lt  i_{k - 1} \le n$$$ such that the $$$ord_{i_j}$$$ lecturer asks for a replacement. Since the $$$ord_{i_j}$$$ lecturer asks for a replacement, it means that from the last replacement at least $$$a_{i_j}$$$ lecturers gave a lecture. In other words, $$$i_{j - 1} + a_{i_j} \le i_j$$$ and $$$1 + a_{i_1} \le i_1$$$.
As a result, we get that $$$1 + a_{i_1} + a_{i_2} + \dots + a_{i_{k-1}} \le i_{k-1} \le n$$$ or $$$a_{i_1} + a_{i_2} + \dots + a_{i_{k-1}}  \lt  n$$$. We can understand that to get $$$k$$$ as big as possible we need to make $$$a_{i_1} + \dots + a_{i_{k - 1}}$$$ as small as possible. So it's optimal to take $$$k$$$ minima possible $$$a_i$$$-s.
If we always take several minima possible $$$a_i$$$-s then if we sort all $$$a_i$$$ we can just find the maximum prefix such that its sum is lower than $$$n$$$. It will be the answer, but we still need to restore the order.
To restore order $$$ord$$$ we can choose these $$$k$$$ minima $$$a_i$$$-s and place all other $$$a_i$$$ between them that there will be exactly $$$a_{i_j} - 1$$$ lecturers between the $$$i_{j - 1}$$$ and $$$i_j$$$ lecturers, $$$a_{i_1}$$$ lecturers before the $$$i_1$$$-th lecturer, and all other lecturers after the $$$i_{k - 1}$$$-th.

solutions.title: Solution (elizarov)
solutions.content:
fun main
()
{
repeat
(
readLine
()!!.
toInt
())
{
val n
=
readLine
()!!.
toInt
()
val a
=
readLine
()!!.
split
(
" "
).
map
{
it
.
toInt
()
}
.
withIndex
().
sortedBy
{
it
.
value
}
var
i
=
0
var
j
=
n
-
1
val answer
=
ArrayList
<
Int
>(
n
)
var
bc
=
0
while
(
i
<=
j
)
{
if
(
bc
>=
a
[
i
].
value
)
{
answer
+=
a
[
i
++].
index
                bc
=
1
}
else
{
answer
+=
a
[
j
--].
index
                bc
++
}
}
println
(
answer
.
joinToString
(
" "
)
{
(
it
+
1
).
toString
()
})
}
}
